medical.title: Could paraxanthine replace caffeine? What we know about the new stimulant appearing in coffee and energy drinks
medical.summary: Paraxanthine, a compound the body naturally produces when it breaks down caffeine, is starting to appear in energy drinks and even some coffee products as a potential caffeine alternative.

medical.title: Largest-ever Parkinson's study shows how symptoms differ between men and women
medical.summary: Parkinson's disease is the fastest growing neurological disorder, with more than 10 million cases worldwide. Up to 150,000 Australians currently live with the disease and 50 new cases are diagnosed each day.

medical.title: Anxiety and ADHD can overlap: Here's how to untangle these widespread mental health disorders
medical.summary: For decades, one of the greatest challenges to treating neurological disorders like attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder is that its symptoms often resemble those of several other conditions. Overlapping disorders are extremely common when it comes to neurological diagnoses.

medical.title: Marathon training: Why hot baths might help you run faster
medical.summary: For decades, elite runners have traveled the world to train at high altitude. When oxygen levels in the air are low, the body responds by producing more red blood cells—the cells responsible for carrying oxygen around the body. When athletes return to sea level, this greater oxygen-carrying capacity can enhance endurance performance.

medical.title: Brain computer interface enables rapid communication for two people with paralysis
medical.summary: Loss of communication can be among the most devastating symptoms for patients with paralysis. A new study by investigators from Mass General Brigham Neuroscience Institute and Brown University describes an investigational implantable brain computer interface (iBCI) typing neuroprosthesis that can restore communication with speed and accuracy.

medical.title: Workshop to examine ethical sharing of research data in Alzheimer's and related dementias
medical.summary: Researchers from academia, federal agencies and national organizations, and participants enrolled in research studies focused on neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease will gather at Georgia State University for a scientific retreat that seeks to advance ethical data and sample sharing practices to accelerate scientific advancements.

medical.title: What went right this week: the good news that matters
medical.summary: Events strengthened the case for renewables, Croatia eliminated landmines, an ‘extinct’ species returned to England, and more The post What went right this week: the good news that matters appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: AI blood test finds silent liver disease years before symptoms
medical.summary: Researchers created an AI-driven liquid biopsy that scans patterns in fragments of DNA circulating in the blood. The system detected early liver fibrosis and cirrhosis—conditions that often go unnoticed until serious damage occurs. By analyzing genome-wide DNA fragmentation patterns rather than specific mutations, the approach captures hidden signals about a person’s overall health. Early detection could help doctors treat liver disease sooner and potentially prevent cancer.

medical.title: Scientists discover a hidden force that helps wire the brain
medical.summary: Growing neurons rely on chemical cues to find their targets, but new research shows that the brain’s physical properties help shape those signals. Scientists discovered that tissue stiffness can trigger the production of guidance molecules through a force-sensing protein called Piezo1. This protein not only detects mechanical forces but also helps maintain the structure of brain tissue. The discovery reveals a powerful link between the brain’s physical environment and how its wiring is built.

medical.title: How a 1930s house was turned into a carbon-negative home
medical.summary: New homes are encouraged to be net-zero ready, but the real work lies in retrofitting Britain’s 29m old properties. One house shows how The post How a 1930s house was turned into a carbon-negative home appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Life lessons: Naz Shah on what life so far has taught her
medical.summary: The British politician on being optimistic, what she learned from her incarcerated mother, and her enduring faith in community The post Life lessons: Naz Shah on what life so far has taught her appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Scientists discover the protein that malaria parasites can’t live without
medical.summary: Scientists have uncovered a crucial weakness in the malaria parasite that could open the door to new treatments. Researchers identified a protein called Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) that acts like a traffic controller during the parasite’s unusual cell division process, ensuring its genetic material is properly separated as it multiplies. When scientists switched off ARK1 in laboratory experiments, the parasite could no longer replicate correctly and failed to complete its life cycle in both humans and mosquitoes—effectively halting its ability to spread.

medical.title: New drug cuts seizures by up to 91% in children with rare epilepsy
medical.summary: A new experimental drug is showing remarkable promise for children with Dravet syndrome, a severe genetic form of epilepsy. In clinical trials, the treatment zorevunersen cut seizures by as much as 91% while also improving quality of life for many patients. The therapy works by boosting the function of a key gene involved in nerve cell signaling. Encouraging results have led researchers to launch a larger Phase 3 trial.

medical.title: A simple hand photo may be the key to detecting a serious disease
medical.summary: Researchers at Kobe University have developed an AI system that can detect acromegaly, a rare hormone disorder, by analyzing photos of the back of the hand and a clenched fist. The disease often develops slowly and can take years to diagnose, even though untreated cases may shorten life expectancy.

medical.title: Inside France’s first LGBTQIA+ senior living residence
medical.summary: France’s first social housing project for LGBTQIA+ seniors has opened in Lyon, offering safety, solidarity and a fresh start The post Inside France’s first LGBTQIA+ senior living residence appeared first on Positive News .

science.title: How a new plan for protein could transform the UK's national security
science.summary: The UK's use of land is indefensibly inefficient. Roughly 5% is used for buildings and roads, 10% for forest and woodland, plus 20% for arable crops. But the largest share, around 50% of our country, is dedicated to livestock.

science.title: Ultrafast laser pulses bring diamond-based quantum internet closer to reality
science.summary: The controlled generation of single photons is an essential element of numerous quantum technology applications, such as quantum networks and quantum computing. A research team has now demonstrated the successful application of the new SUPER (Swing-UP of the quantum EmitteR population) method. The approach facilitates the controlled generation of light particles (photons). Results of the study were recently published in the journal Nature Communications.

science.title: Researchers develop biodegradable, plant‑based packaging from natural fibers
science.summary: Jie Wu, an engineering graduate student, was studying a type of striking white beetle found in Southeast Asia and attempting to figure out how to mimic its brilliant color when an unexpected discovery upended the experiment.

science.title: Ethnic land rights fail to provide Afro-Colombians with economic security
science.summary: The legal rights designed to protect Afro-Colombian communities are not lifting them out of economic precarity—and are leaving them vulnerable to the illegal drug trade and illicit mining as a result—according to new research from The University of Manchester.

science.title: Superconductor advancement could unlock ultra-energy-efficient electronics
science.summary: Superconducting materials could play a crucial role in the energy-efficient applications of the future. However, several technical challenges still stand in the way of their practical use. Now, researchers at Chalmers University of Technology in Sweden have developed a new material design that addresses a major obstacle in the field: enabling superconductivity to operate at higher temperatures while also withstanding strong magnetic fields. This breakthrough could pave the way for far more energy-efficient electronics and quantum technologies.

science.title: New study complicates the search for alien oxygen
science.summary: Oxygen has been the most important gas in our search for life among the cosmos thus far. On Earth, we have it in abundance because it is produced by biological synthesis. But that might not be the case on other planets, so even if we do find a very clear high oxygen signal in the atmosphere of an exoplanet, it might not be a clear indication that life exists there. A new paper, available in pre-print on arXiv, by Margaret Turcotte Seavey and a team of researchers from institutions like the NASA Goddard Space Flight Center and Johns Hopkins University, adds some additional context to what else might be going on in those atmospheres. In particular, they note that if there's even a little bit of water vapor, it can make a big difference in whether a lifeless rock looks like a living, thriving world.

science.title: Astronomers discover long-period radio transient of unknown origin
science.summary: Using the Australian SKA Pathfinder (ASKAP), astronomers have discovered a new long-period radio transient source, which received the designation ASKAP J142431.2–612611 (ASKAP J1424 for short). The newfound transient has a period of approximately 36 minutes and its nature is unknown. The finding was reported in a paper published March 9 on the arXiv preprint server.

science.title: Criminology professor co-authors study examining stereotypes against Asian women
science.summary: The Asian community in the United States was microscopic six decades ago, for the wrong reasons. Because of discriminatory policies put in place that targeted the group—including federal legislation first enacted in the 19th century—Asian Americans in 1965 made up less than 1% of the U.S.'s total population, according to the Pew Research Center.

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Ailing “Megaberg” Sparks Surge of Microscopic Life
environment.summary: As Iceberg A-23A disintegrated, it shed meltwater that helped fuel an extensive phytoplankton bloom in the South Atlantic Ocean. The post Ailing “Megaberg” Sparks Surge of Microscopic Life appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Humpback whale recovery is changing who fathers the calves
environment.summary: A new study shows that as humpback whale populations recover from past whaling, older males are gaining a major advantage in reproduction. Early in the recovery, breeding groups were dominated by younger whales. But as more mature males returned, they increasingly fathered more calves than their younger rivals. Scientists say experience in singing and competing may help older males win the breeding battle.

environment.title: Ocean temperatures may be protecting Earth from a planet-wide drought
environment.summary: Ocean temperatures may be quietly protecting the world from a global drought catastrophe. By analyzing more than a century of climate data, researchers discovered that droughts rarely spread across the planet at the same time, affecting only about 1.8%–6.5% of global land simultaneously—far less than earlier estimates. The reason lies largely in shifting ocean patterns such as El Niño and La Niña, which create a patchwork of drought conditions across continents instead of one massive worldwide dry spell.

environment.title: T. rex took 40 years to reach full size, study finds
environment.summary: Tyrannosaurus rex may have taken far longer to grow up than scientists once thought. By analyzing growth rings in fossilized leg bones from 17 tyrannosaur specimens and using new statistical methods, researchers found that the famous predator likely took about 40 years to reach its full size—around eight tons—rather than the previously estimated 25 years.

environment.title: Scientists just looked inside Darwin’s 200-year-old specimen jars without opening them
environment.summary: Scientists have used a laser technique to analyze Charles Darwin’s original Galápagos specimens without opening their nearly 200-year-old jars. By shining light through the glass, the method reveals the chemical makeup of the preservation fluids inside. Researchers successfully identified the contents in most samples, offering new clues about historical preservation practices. The breakthrough could help museums protect millions of delicate specimens without risking damage.

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: A Little Town With a Long Name
environment.summary: A NASA luminary from the Apollo era grew up in Wales near Llanfairpwllgwyngyllgogerychwyrndrobwllllantysiliogogogoch. The post A Little Town With a Long Name appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: 2700-year-old teeth reveal the hidden lives of Iron Age Italians
environment.summary: Iron Age teeth from southern Italy have become time capsules, preserving intimate details of childhood and diet. Growth lines in the enamel reveal moments of early-life stress, while hardened plaque holds microscopic remains of cereals, legumes, and fermented foods. The findings suggest a community with diverse food resources and strong Mediterranean connections. Even a small sample offers a striking glimpse into how people lived, grew, and ate nearly three millennia ago.

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Searching for Selenite
environment.summary: Oklahoma’s Salt Plains National Wildlife Refuge attracts rare and diverse species—and enthusiasts looking for a distinct type of crystallized gypsum. The post Searching for Selenite appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Blasted off Mars and still alive
environment.summary: A famously resilient bacterium may be tough enough to survive one of the most violent events imaginable on Mars. In laboratory experiments designed to mimic the crushing shock of a massive asteroid impact, researchers squeezed Deinococcus radiodurans between steel plates and blasted it with pressures reaching 3 GPa (30,000 times atmospheric pressure). Even under these extreme conditions, a significant portion of the microbes survived.

environment.title: The hidden technology that could unlock commercial fusion power
environment.summary: Fusion energy may be one of the most promising clean power sources of the future—but only if scientists can precisely measure the extreme, fast-moving plasmas that make it possible. A new U.S. Department of Energy–sponsored report urges major investment in advanced diagnostic tools—the high-tech “sensors” that track plasma temperature, density, and behavior inside fusion systems. Bringing together 70 experts from universities, national labs, and private industry, the workshop identified seven priority areas ranging from burning plasma to full-scale pilot plants.

environment.title: For every known vertebrate species, two more may be hiding in plain sight
environment.summary: Earth’s vertebrate diversity may be far richer than anyone realized. A sweeping analysis of more than 300 studies suggests that for every known fish, bird, reptile, amphibian, or mammal species, there are about two nearly identical “cryptic” species hiding in plain sight—genetically distinct but visually almost impossible to tell apart. Thanks to advances in DNA sequencing, scientists are uncovering these long-separated lineages, some evolving independently for over a million years.

environment.title: Ancient mystery on K’gari: World’s largest sand island lakes dried up during rainy era
environment.summary: K’gari’s iconic lakes have existed for tens of thousands of years—but they haven’t always been full. New research shows that about 7,500 years ago, during a time of high rainfall, several of the island’s deepest lakes mysteriously vanished. Scientists believe changing wind patterns may have redirected rain away from the island. As the climate shifts again, the lakes’ long-term survival is no longer guaranteed.

environment.title: Atacama surprise: The world’s driest desert is teeming with hidden life
environment.summary: Even in the ultra-dry Atacama Desert, tiny soil-dwelling nematodes are thriving in surprising diversity. Scientists found that biodiversity increases with moisture and altitude shapes which species survive. In the most extreme zones, many nematodes reproduce asexually — a possible survival advantage. The discovery suggests that life in arid regions may be far richer, and more fragile, than once believed.

environment.title: Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars
environment.summary: The hill-shaped features are a sign of explosive volcanic activity—a rarity on the Red Planet. The post Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Massive asteroid impact 6.3 million years ago left giant glass field in Brazil
environment.summary: For the first time ever, scientists have uncovered a vast field of tektites in Brazil — mysterious glassy fragments forged when a powerful extraterrestrial object slammed into Earth about 6.3 million years ago. Named “geraisites” after Minas Gerais, where they were first found, these dark, aerodynamic droplets of natural glass stretch across more than 900 kilometers and may mark one of South America’s most significant ancient impact events.

environment.title: Scientists just created chocolate honey packed with surprising health perks
environment.summary: Scientists in Brazil have transformed cocoa waste into a functional chocolate-infused honey packed with antioxidants and natural stimulants. Using ultrasound waves, they enhanced honey’s ability to pull beneficial compounds from cocoa shells—no synthetic solvents required. The process is considered green and sustainable, and the product could find its way into gourmet foods and cosmetics.

environment.title: Jupiter’s moons may have formed with the ingredients for life
environment.summary: Jupiter’s icy moons may have been seeded with the chemical ingredients for life from the very beginning. An international team of scientists modeled how complex organic molecules—essential building blocks for biology—could have formed in the swirling disk of gas and dust around the young Sun and later been carried into Jupiter’s own moon-forming disk. Their results suggest that up to half of the icy material that built moons like Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto may have delivered freshly made organic compounds without being chemically destroyed.

environment.title: How the body really ages: 7 million cells mapped across 21 organs
environment.summary: Scientists have built a massive cellular atlas showing how aging reshapes the body across 21 organs. Studying nearly 7 million cells, they found that aging starts earlier than expected and unfolds in a coordinated way throughout the body. About a quarter of cell types change in number over time, and many of these shifts differ between males and females. The research also highlights shared genetic “hotspots” that could become targets for anti-aging therapies.

environment.title: Scientists discover a bacterial kill switch and it could change the fight against superbugs
environment.summary: Drug-resistant bacteria are becoming harder to treat, pushing scientists to look for new antibiotic targets. Researchers have now discovered that several unrelated viruses disable a key bacterial protein called MurJ, which is essential for building the bacterial cell wall. High-resolution imaging shows these viral proteins lock MurJ into a single position, stopping cell wall construction and leading to bacterial death.

environment.title: Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice
environment.summary: Nearly 50 years ago, the first Landsat satellite captured the rare sight of Mid-Atlantic waterways frozen over. The post Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations
environment.summary: Description NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover discovered these bumpy, pea-sized nodules while exploring a region filled with boxwork formations — low ridges standing roughly 3 to 6 feet (1 to 2 meters) tall with sandy hollows in-between. This mosaic is made up of 50 individual images taken by Curiosity’s Mars Hand Lens Imager (MAHLI), a camera on […] The post Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Descoperă cine sunt câștigătorii Rubik Garage – HealthTech Edition la Demo Day-ul sold-out din Milano

mix_items.summary: Dani Mocanu n-a reuşit să scape de puşcărie – recursul în casaţie i-a fost respins, iar manelistul rămâne cu pedeapsa de 4 ani pentru tentativă de omor. De îndată ce a ieşit cu maşina din tunel, şefa ICCJ, Lia Savonea, a oferit şi motivarea pentru decizia Curţii: „Scuze! Astăzi, în jurul orei 11, în Piaţa Romană din Capitală a fost rostit pentru prima oară „Bossule, ai şi tu o Terea?” de către o persoană cu alură de boschetar. Potrivit martorilor, cuvintele ultramoderne îi aparţin lui Mariusică, un hipiot lăţos care îşi face veacul prin Romană […] The post România intră oficial în secolul 21. În magazinele săteşti, farmaciile, fast-foodurile şi chioşcurile spirt din judeţ se vinde zilele acestea o ediţie limitată a celebrei Mona, colorată în verde strălucitor. Lansarea alcoolului sanitar verde are loc cu ocazia zilei de Sf. Patrick, ocrotitorul Irlandei, ţară insulară poreclită uneori „Vasluiul […] The post E St. Patrick’s Day şi la Vaslui! mix_items.summary: O idee pornită din dorința de a dezvolta competențele de leadership ale elevilor s-a transformat, în doar câțiva ani, într-o inițiativă educațională care conectează școli din mai multe țări europene. Povestea proiectului „Leadership HUB” începe cu un proiect finanțat de Teach for Romania și continuă astăzi prin colaborări internaționale în cadrul platformei eTwinning și al […]

mix_items.title: How a new plan for protein could transform the UK's national security
mix_items.summary: The UK's use of land is indefensibly inefficient. Roughly 5% is used for buildings and roads, 10% for forest and woodland, plus 20% for arable crops. But the largest share, around 50% of our country, is dedicated to livestock.

mix_items.title: 4 ani de „Start în educație”, cel mai mare program de educație timpurie din România: 1 din 3 grădinițe este dotată cu resurse educaționale.
mix_items.summary: Cel mai mare program de educație timpurie din România ajunge la 240.000 de copii preșcolari, 50.000 de părinți, 9.000 de... Articolul 4 ani de „Start în educație”, cel mai mare program de educație timpurie din România: 1 din 3 grădinițe este dotată cu resurse educaționale. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Articolul Programul de educație nutrițională, NutriCOOLtura, și-a dublat impactul în 2025 și a ajuns în și mai multe școli din țară apare prima dată în United Way Romania .

mix_items.title: Ultrafast laser pulses bring diamond-based quantum internet closer to reality
mix_items.summary: The controlled generation of single photons is an essential element of numerous quantum technology applications, such as quantum networks and quantum computing. A research team has now demonstrated the successful application of the new SUPER (Swing-UP of the quantum EmitteR population) method. The approach facilitates the controlled generation of light particles (photons). Results of the study were recently published in the journal Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: 36 de artiști ai noii generații din România și Republica Moldova selectați  ca bursieri și promotori ai artelor vizuale și muzicii clasice
mix_items.summary: A 18-a generație de bursieri ai programului național Tinere Talente susținut de Fundația Regală Margareta a României Fundația Regală Margareta a României anunță cei 36 de tineri artiști din România și Republica Moldova care vor beneficia de burse în cadrul ediției a 18-a a... Post-ul 36 de artiști ai noii generații din România și Republica Moldova selectați ca bursieri și promotori ai artelor vizuale și muzicii clasice apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Researchers develop biodegradable, plant‑based packaging from natural fibers
mix_items.summary: Jie Wu, an engineering graduate student, was studying a type of striking white beetle found in Southeast Asia and attempting to figure out how to mimic its brilliant color when an unexpected discovery upended the experiment.

mix_items.title: Ethnic land rights fail to provide Afro-Colombians with economic security
mix_items.summary: The legal rights designed to protect Afro-Colombian communities are not lifting them out of economic precarity—and are leaving them vulnerable to the illegal drug trade and illicit mining as a result—according to new research from The University of Manchester.

mix_items.title: Superconductor advancement could unlock ultra-energy-efficient electronics
mix_items.summary: Superconducting materials could play a crucial role in the energy-efficient applications of the future. However, several technical challenges still stand in the way of their practical use. Now, researchers at Chalmers University of Technology in Sweden have developed a new material design that addresses a major obstacle in the field: enabling superconductivity to operate at higher temperatures while also withstanding strong magnetic fields. This breakthrough could pave the way for far more energy-efficient electronics and quantum technologies.

mix_items.title: New study complicates the search for alien oxygen
mix_items.summary: Oxygen has been the most important gas in our search for life among the cosmos thus far. On Earth, we have it in abundance because it is produced by biological synthesis. But that might not be the case on other planets, so even if we do find a very clear high oxygen signal in the atmosphere of an exoplanet, it might not be a clear indication that life exists there. A new paper, available in pre-print on arXiv, by Margaret Turcotte Seavey and a team of researchers from institutions like the NASA Goddard Space Flight Center and Johns Hopkins University, adds some additional context to what else might be going on in those atmospheres. In particular, they note that if there's even a little bit of water vapor, it can make a big difference in whether a lifeless rock looks like a living, thriving world.

mix_items.title: Astronomers discover long-period radio transient of unknown origin
mix_items.summary: Using the Australian SKA Pathfinder (ASKAP), astronomers have discovered a new long-period radio transient source, which received the designation ASKAP J142431.2–612611 (ASKAP J1424 for short). The newfound transient has a period of approximately 36 minutes and its nature is unknown. The finding was reported in a paper published March 9 on the arXiv preprint server.

mix_items.title: Criminology professor co-authors study examining stereotypes against Asian women
mix_items.summary: The Asian community in the United States was microscopic six decades ago, for the wrong reasons. Because of discriminatory policies put in place that targeted the group—including federal legislation first enacted in the 19th century—Asian Americans in 1965 made up less than 1% of the U.S.'s total population, according to the Pew Research Center.

mix_items.title: Could paraxanthine replace caffeine? What we know about the new stimulant appearing in coffee and energy drinks
mix_items.summary: Paraxanthine, a compound the body naturally produces when it breaks down caffeine, is starting to appear in energy drinks and even some coffee products as a potential caffeine alternative.

mix_items.title: Largest-ever Parkinson's study shows how symptoms differ between men and women
mix_items.summary: Parkinson's disease is the fastest growing neurological disorder, with more than 10 million cases worldwide. Up to 150,000 Australians currently live with the disease and 50 new cases are diagnosed each day.

mix_items.title: Anxiety and ADHD can overlap: Here's how to untangle these widespread mental health disorders
mix_items.summary: For decades, one of the greatest challenges to treating neurological disorders like attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder is that its symptoms often resemble those of several other conditions. Overlapping disorders are extremely common when it comes to neurological diagnoses.

mix_items.title: Marathon training: Why hot baths might help you run faster
mix_items.summary: For decades, elite runners have traveled the world to train at high altitude. When oxygen levels in the air are low, the body responds by producing more red blood cells—the cells responsible for carrying oxygen around the body. When athletes return to sea level, this greater oxygen-carrying capacity can enhance endurance performance.

mix_items.title: Brain computer interface enables rapid communication for two people with paralysis
mix_items.summary: Loss of communication can be among the most devastating symptoms for patients with paralysis. A new study by investigators from Mass General Brigham Neuroscience Institute and Brown University describes an investigational implantable brain computer interface (iBCI) typing neuroprosthesis that can restore communication with speed and accuracy.

mix_items.title: Workshop to examine ethical sharing of research data in Alzheimer's and related dementias
mix_items.summary: Researchers from academia, federal agencies and national organizations, and participants enrolled in research studies focused on neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease will gather at Georgia State University for a scientific retreat that seeks to advance ethical data and sample sharing practices to accelerate scientific advancements.

mix_items.title: What went right this week: the good news that matters
mix_items.summary: Events strengthened the case for renewables, Croatia eliminated landmines, an ‘extinct’ species returned to England, and more The post What went right this week: the good news that matters appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: AI blood test finds silent liver disease years before symptoms
mix_items.summary: Researchers created an AI-driven liquid biopsy that scans patterns in fragments of DNA circulating in the blood. The system detected early liver fibrosis and cirrhosis—conditions that often go unnoticed until serious damage occurs. By analyzing genome-wide DNA fragmentation patterns rather than specific mutations, the approach captures hidden signals about a person’s overall health. Early detection could help doctors treat liver disease sooner and potentially prevent cancer.

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Ailing “Megaberg” Sparks Surge of Microscopic Life
mix_items.summary: As Iceberg A-23A disintegrated, it shed meltwater that helped fuel an extensive phytoplankton bloom in the South Atlantic Ocean. The post Ailing “Megaberg” Sparks Surge of Microscopic Life appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Humpback whale recovery is changing who fathers the calves
mix_items.summary: A new study shows that as humpback whale populations recover from past whaling, older males are gaining a major advantage in reproduction. Early in the recovery, breeding groups were dominated by younger whales. But as more mature males returned, they increasingly fathered more calves than their younger rivals. Scientists say experience in singing and competing may help older males win the breeding battle.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a hidden force that helps wire the brain
mix_items.summary: Growing neurons rely on chemical cues to find their targets, but new research shows that the brain’s physical properties help shape those signals. Scientists discovered that tissue stiffness can trigger the production of guidance molecules through a force-sensing protein called Piezo1. This protein not only detects mechanical forces but also helps maintain the structure of brain tissue. The discovery reveals a powerful link between the brain’s physical environment and how its wiring is built.

mix_items.title: Ocean temperatures may be protecting Earth from a planet-wide drought
mix_items.summary: Ocean temperatures may be quietly protecting the world from a global drought catastrophe. By analyzing more than a century of climate data, researchers discovered that droughts rarely spread across the planet at the same time, affecting only about 1.8%–6.5% of global land simultaneously—far less than earlier estimates. The reason lies largely in shifting ocean patterns such as El Niño and La Niña, which create a patchwork of drought conditions across continents instead of one massive worldwide dry spell.

mix_items.title: T. rex took 40 years to reach full size, study finds
mix_items.summary: Tyrannosaurus rex may have taken far longer to grow up than scientists once thought. By analyzing growth rings in fossilized leg bones from 17 tyrannosaur specimens and using new statistical methods, researchers found that the famous predator likely took about 40 years to reach its full size—around eight tons—rather than the previously estimated 25 years.

mix_items.title: How a 1930s house was turned into a carbon-negative home
mix_items.summary: New homes are encouraged to be net-zero ready, but the real work lies in retrofitting Britain’s 29m old properties. One house shows how The post How a 1930s house was turned into a carbon-negative home appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Life lessons: Naz Shah on what life so far has taught her
mix_items.summary: The British politician on being optimistic, what she learned from her incarcerated mother, and her enduring faith in community The post Life lessons: Naz Shah on what life so far has taught her appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Scientists just looked inside Darwin’s 200-year-old specimen jars without opening them
mix_items.summary: Scientists have used a laser technique to analyze Charles Darwin’s original Galápagos specimens without opening their nearly 200-year-old jars. By shining light through the glass, the method reveals the chemical makeup of the preservation fluids inside. Researchers successfully identified the contents in most samples, offering new clues about historical preservation practices. The breakthrough could help museums protect millions of delicate specimens without risking damage.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover the protein that malaria parasites can’t live without
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a crucial weakness in the malaria parasite that could open the door to new treatments. Researchers identified a protein called Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) that acts like a traffic controller during the parasite’s unusual cell division process, ensuring its genetic material is properly separated as it multiplies. When scientists switched off ARK1 in laboratory experiments, the parasite could no longer replicate correctly and failed to complete its life cycle in both humans and mosquitoes—effectively halting its ability to spread.

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: A Little Town With a Long Name
mix_items.summary: A NASA luminary from the Apollo era grew up in Wales near Llanfairpwllgwyngyllgogerychwyrndrobwllllantysiliogogogoch. The post A Little Town With a Long Name appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: New drug cuts seizures by up to 91% in children with rare epilepsy
mix_items.summary: A new experimental drug is showing remarkable promise for children with Dravet syndrome, a severe genetic form of epilepsy. In clinical trials, the treatment zorevunersen cut seizures by as much as 91% while also improving quality of life for many patients. The therapy works by boosting the function of a key gene involved in nerve cell signaling. Encouraging results have led researchers to launch a larger Phase 3 trial.

mix_items.title: 2700-year-old teeth reveal the hidden lives of Iron Age Italians
mix_items.summary: Iron Age teeth from southern Italy have become time capsules, preserving intimate details of childhood and diet. Growth lines in the enamel reveal moments of early-life stress, while hardened plaque holds microscopic remains of cereals, legumes, and fermented foods. The findings suggest a community with diverse food resources and strong Mediterranean connections. Even a small sample offers a striking glimpse into how people lived, grew, and ate nearly three millennia ago.

mix_items.title: A simple hand photo may be the key to detecting a serious disease
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Kobe University have developed an AI system that can detect acromegaly, a rare hormone disorder, by analyzing photos of the back of the hand and a clenched fist. The disease often develops slowly and can take years to diagnose, even though untreated cases may shorten life expectancy.

mix_items.title: Inside France’s first LGBTQIA+ senior living residence
mix_items.summary: France’s first social housing project for LGBTQIA+ seniors has opened in Lyon, offering safety, solidarity and a fresh start The post Inside France’s first LGBTQIA+ senior living residence appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Searching for Selenite
mix_items.summary: Oklahoma’s Salt Plains National Wildlife Refuge attracts rare and diverse species—and enthusiasts looking for a distinct type of crystallized gypsum. The post Searching for Selenite appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Blasted off Mars and still alive
mix_items.summary: A famously resilient bacterium may be tough enough to survive one of the most violent events imaginable on Mars. In laboratory experiments designed to mimic the crushing shock of a massive asteroid impact, researchers squeezed Deinococcus radiodurans between steel plates and blasted it with pressures reaching 3 GPa (30,000 times atmospheric pressure). Even under these extreme conditions, a significant portion of the microbes survived.

mix_items.title: The hidden technology that could unlock commercial fusion power
mix_items.summary: Fusion energy may be one of the most promising clean power sources of the future—but only if scientists can precisely measure the extreme, fast-moving plasmas that make it possible. A new U.S. Department of Energy–sponsored report urges major investment in advanced diagnostic tools—the high-tech “sensors” that track plasma temperature, density, and behavior inside fusion systems. Bringing together 70 experts from universities, national labs, and private industry, the workshop identified seven priority areas ranging from burning plasma to full-scale pilot plants.

mix_items.title: For every known vertebrate species, two more may be hiding in plain sight
mix_items.summary: Earth’s vertebrate diversity may be far richer than anyone realized. A sweeping analysis of more than 300 studies suggests that for every known fish, bird, reptile, amphibian, or mammal species, there are about two nearly identical “cryptic” species hiding in plain sight—genetically distinct but visually almost impossible to tell apart. Thanks to advances in DNA sequencing, scientists are uncovering these long-separated lineages, some evolving independently for over a million years.

mix_items.title: Ancient mystery on K’gari: World’s largest sand island lakes dried up during rainy era
mix_items.summary: K’gari’s iconic lakes have existed for tens of thousands of years—but they haven’t always been full. New research shows that about 7,500 years ago, during a time of high rainfall, several of the island’s deepest lakes mysteriously vanished. Scientists believe changing wind patterns may have redirected rain away from the island. As the climate shifts again, the lakes’ long-term survival is no longer guaranteed.

mix_items.title: Atacama surprise: The world’s driest desert is teeming with hidden life
mix_items.summary: Even in the ultra-dry Atacama Desert, tiny soil-dwelling nematodes are thriving in surprising diversity. Scientists found that biodiversity increases with moisture and altitude shapes which species survive. In the most extreme zones, many nematodes reproduce asexually — a possible survival advantage. The discovery suggests that life in arid regions may be far richer, and more fragile, than once believed.

mix_items.title: Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars
mix_items.summary: The hill-shaped features are a sign of explosive volcanic activity—a rarity on the Red Planet. The post Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Massive asteroid impact 6.3 million years ago left giant glass field in Brazil
mix_items.summary: For the first time ever, scientists have uncovered a vast field of tektites in Brazil — mysterious glassy fragments forged when a powerful extraterrestrial object slammed into Earth about 6.3 million years ago. Named “geraisites” after Minas Gerais, where they were first found, these dark, aerodynamic droplets of natural glass stretch across more than 900 kilometers and may mark one of South America’s most significant ancient impact events.

mix_items.title: Scientists just created chocolate honey packed with surprising health perks
mix_items.summary: Scientists in Brazil have transformed cocoa waste into a functional chocolate-infused honey packed with antioxidants and natural stimulants. Using ultrasound waves, they enhanced honey’s ability to pull beneficial compounds from cocoa shells—no synthetic solvents required. The process is considered green and sustainable, and the product could find its way into gourmet foods and cosmetics.

mix_items.title: Jupiter’s moons may have formed with the ingredients for life
mix_items.summary: Jupiter’s icy moons may have been seeded with the chemical ingredients for life from the very beginning. An international team of scientists modeled how complex organic molecules—essential building blocks for biology—could have formed in the swirling disk of gas and dust around the young Sun and later been carried into Jupiter’s own moon-forming disk. Their results suggest that up to half of the icy material that built moons like Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto may have delivered freshly made organic compounds without being chemically destroyed.

mix_items.title: How the body really ages: 7 million cells mapped across 21 organs
mix_items.summary: Scientists have built a massive cellular atlas showing how aging reshapes the body across 21 organs. Studying nearly 7 million cells, they found that aging starts earlier than expected and unfolds in a coordinated way throughout the body. About a quarter of cell types change in number over time, and many of these shifts differ between males and females. The research also highlights shared genetic “hotspots” that could become targets for anti-aging therapies.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a bacterial kill switch and it could change the fight against superbugs
mix_items.summary: Drug-resistant bacteria are becoming harder to treat, pushing scientists to look for new antibiotic targets. Researchers have now discovered that several unrelated viruses disable a key bacterial protein called MurJ, which is essential for building the bacterial cell wall. High-resolution imaging shows these viral proteins lock MurJ into a single position, stopping cell wall construction and leading to bacterial death.

mix_items.title: Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice
mix_items.summary: Nearly 50 years ago, the first Landsat satellite captured the rare sight of Mid-Atlantic waterways frozen over. The post Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations
mix_items.summary: Description NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover discovered these bumpy, pea-sized nodules while exploring a region filled with boxwork formations — low ridges standing roughly 3 to 6 feet (1 to 2 meters) tall with sandy hollows in-between. This mosaic is made up of 50 individual images taken by Curiosity’s Mars Hand Lens Imager (MAHLI), a camera on […] The post Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations appeared first on NASA Science .

joke_ro.text: Cafeaua mea are acum rol de team lead.
